story:
I’m in charge. Ask anyone. My loyal subjects answer my every need. If I complain, they rush in; if I look like I might complain, they fix it faster. They know I’m running things, and I try not to take advantage. I even show appreciation now and then.

From my earliest memory there were two subjects always beside me. Sometimes both, sometimes one. If my tummy felt empty or my world felt wrong, I complained as loud as I could, and they bent over backwards. Food? New surroundings? Soothing? Anything to stop the noise. Before long, they trained themselves to a schedule so I wouldn’t get so uncomfortable. We all slept better. That’s when they clearly understood I’m in charge.

Then they started bringing in more subjects. I didn’t ask for that. These others wiggled their faces at me, hoping for strange responses. I didn’t understand, so I complained. A lot. Eventually, they either tried less hard or I played along. A smile here and there worked like magic. I learned to recognize the frequent visitors. My favorites sometimes took me out in the vroom, vroom, bundled me in a bucket, and handed me to one or two of these others. I didn’t like being passed around, so I let them know who’s in charge. Still, some learned to be entertaining, so I tolerated them. I even did tricks: wiggled fingers, blew kisses. They loved it, and then I got to leave with my favorites. Some were scary at first—until I proved my authority.

I grew from staring up at everything to rolling over, scooting to reach bright objects, and tasting the world. Exploring made me very happy. Then I met a terrible new word: “No, no!” I heard it when I climbed uneven places, when I went for pretty things on tables and shelves. I complained, but it didn’t always work. They moved some treasures where I couldn’t reach them and left others where I could, expecting me to know the difference. A fierce battle of wills began. It’s my hardest challenge yet.

The vroom, vroom takes me to different worlds. I get clicked into my bucket. I learned to make it click on my own. Oh, the places we go. Once we found giant, pretty hills. Ada put me in a bucket on his back so I was as tall as he was, and we climbed, climbed, climbed. My subjects call that “adventure.” Gma gave me a long stick with a foot to chase a ball into a hole. Dia, who looks like Ama but not as tall, makes me laugh and shows me pictures of horses and dogs. Ama wants real words, not “horsey” or “doggy.” Dia rubs a silly-shaped box with a long stick and pretty music comes out. I want to touch it. “No, no,” again. GG gave me my own box and stick, but it doesn’t work like hers, so I drum it on the floor instead.

I carry small vroom, vrooms wherever we go in the big one. I even ride one. GG gave me a little subject for my stroller vroom, vroom. I clicked it in. It clicked out. I told it, “No, no!”

They say I’m in my terrible twos. I don’t think so. I don’t complain nearly as much as I used to. And as long as they secretly remember I’m in charge, we’ll get along just fine.

(Ama = Mama; Ada = Dada; Gma = Grandma; Dia = Aunt Lydia; GG = Great-Grandma; Iam = Liam.)
